title: Notification System — System Design
description: Designing a system that delivers notifications — push, email, SMS, in-app — reliably and at scale, without spamming users or losing messages. We build from a single synchronous send toward a queued, deduplicated, preference-aware, multi-channel fanout platform, treating reliable asynchronous delivery and user control as the core problems.

nodes.label: Notification API
nodes.description: Notification API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Client / Service
nodes.description: Client / Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Notifications DB
nodes.description: Notifications DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Email Provider
nodes.description: Email Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Fanout Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Idempotency / Dedup Store
nodes.description: Idempotency / Dedup Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Rate Limiter / Throttler
nodes.description: Rate Limiter / Throttler: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Preferences Store
nodes.description: Preferences Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Preference Service
nodes.description: Preference Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Push/Email/SMS Provider
nodes.description: Push/Email/SMS Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: APNs / FCM
nodes.description: APNs / FCM: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Message Queue
nodes.description: Message Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Bulk Lane (tenant-partitioned)
nodes.description: Bulk Lane (tenant-partitioned): Lower-priority lane for marketing/blast traffic, partitioned by tenant so one noisy tenant can't starve others within the lane.

nodes.label: Urgent Lane (tenant-partitioned)
nodes.description: Urgent Lane (tenant-partitioned): High-priority lane for OTP/security/time-sensitive traffic that bypasses bulk; also partitioned by tenant inside the lane.

nodes.label: Channel Router
nodes.description: Channel Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: SMS Primary
nodes.description: SMS Primary: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: SMS Secondary
nodes.description: SMS Secondary: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: SMS Provider
nodes.description: SMS Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Template Service
nodes.description: Template Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Subscriptions / Topics
nodes.description: Subscriptions / Topics: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Delivery Worker
nodes.description: Delivery Worker: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Limiter / Digest Store
nodes.description: Limiter / Digest Store: Fast shared counter store (e.g. Redis) holding token-bucket counters per user/tenant/category and digest accumulation windows.

nodes.label: Provider Callback / Receipt API
nodes.description: Provider Callback / Receipt API: Inbound webhook endpoint that ingests asynchronous delivery receipts, bounces, and complaints from providers.

nodes.label: Receipt / Status Worker
nodes.description: Receipt / Status Worker: Reconciles provider callbacks into delivery status and updates contact-point health (invalid tokens, bounces, complaints).

nodes.label: Multi-Channel Provider (SaaS)
nodes.description: Multi-Channel Provider (SaaS): A single third-party platform that delivers across push/email/SMS behind one API, trading control for less integration work.

functional: Accept a notification request from internal services (e.g. 'order shipped').
Deliver across multiple channels: push, email, SMS, in-app.
Respect per-user preferences and opt-outs per channel and category.
Fan out one event to many recipients (e.g. a broadcast or a followed topic).
Track delivery status and expose it to senders.
nonFunctional: Reliable delivery: an accepted notification reaches a terminal state — delivered, suppressed, or explicitly failed — and is never silently dropped.
At-least-once processing with deduplication: minimize duplicate user-visible sends within a bounded window (exactly-once across third-party providers is not guaranteed).
Low latency for time-sensitive notifications (OTP, security alerts).
High throughput with bursty load (marketing blasts, breaking news).
Do not spam: rate-limit per user and collapse duplicates.
Graceful degradation when a downstream provider is slow or down.

capacity.label: Notification throughput
capacity.note: Marketing blasts and breaking-news fanout spike far above average.

capacity.label: Recipients per broadcast
capacity.note: One event can fan out to millions of subscribers.

capacity.label: Delivery units per event
capacity.note: The real work unit. A 10M-recipient, 2-channel broadcast is ~20M delivery rows/queue messages; retries and receipts add more write streams on top.

capacity.label: Channels
capacity.note: Each has a different provider, latency, and failure profile.

capacity.label: Delivery latency target
capacity.note: OTP/security alerts are urgent; marketing can be minutes.

capacity.label: Dedup window
capacity.note: Retries and duplicate events must not double-notify within the window.

capacity.label: Retention of delivery status
capacity.note: For debugging, analytics, and user-facing 'delivered' state.

api.description: Accept a notification for delivery. Idempotency-Key dedupes retries of the same logical event. `category` and `priority` drive preferences, quiet hours, and the urgent/bulk lane; `locale` selects the template variant; `tenant_id` scopes quotas and partitioning; `target` can be explicit users, a topic, or a segment (the fanout phase expands it).
Fetch the logical notification's aggregate status — a roll-up over all per-recipient, per-channel deliveries, not a single delivery's state.
Paginated per-recipient, per-channel delivery rows for one notification — the granular view a sender needs since one POST can target millions of recipients.
Set per-channel, per-category notification preferences and quiet hours.
Subscribe a user to a topic for fanout (e.g. a channel they follow).

dataModel.name: notifications
dataModel.note: One row per accepted logical event. Holds routing metadata; the per-recipient/channel state lives in `deliveries`.

dataModel.name: deliveries
dataModel.note: One row per (notification, recipient, channel); the unit workers act on and the delivery state machine: queued -> sent -> delivered | bounced | failed | suppressed | expired.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(queued, sent, delivered, bounced, failed, suppressed, expired)

fields.name: suppression_reason
fields.type: varchar null (opt_out, quiet_hours, rate_limit, bounced, invalid_token)

dataModel.name: delivery_attempts
dataModel.note: Append-only history of each provider attempt for a delivery — feeds retries, debugging, and provider-error analytics.

dataModel.name: templates
dataModel.note: Versioned, per-channel, per-locale content. Workers render the pinned version so a mid-flight template edit can't change an in-flight broadcast.

dataModel.name: contact_points
dataModel.note: Per-user, per-channel delivery address (push token, email, phone) plus its health — invalid tokens/bounced addresses get suppressed from provider feedback.

dataModel.name: provider_callbacks
dataModel.note: Raw async receipts/webhooks from providers, keyed by provider_message_id; the receipt worker reconciles these into `deliveries` status.

dataModel.name: preferences
dataModel.note: Per-user, per-category, per-channel opt-in/out plus quiet hours.

dataModel.name: dedup_keys
dataModel.note: Idempotency keys for accepted events; suppresses duplicate enqueue.

tradeoff: Self-host a log (Kafka/Pulsar/Redpanda) when you want replay, ordered partitions, and high fanout throughput and can run the broker.
Self-host a work queue (RabbitMQ/NATS) for simple per-message ack, visibility timeouts, and a built-in DLQ.
Go managed (SQS/Pub/Sub/Service Bus, or MSK/Event Hubs) to drop broker ops — at the cost of per-partition throughput caps and per-message cost at blast scale.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed queue with native DLQ, visibility timeouts, and autoscaling consumers removes most of the hand-built retry/lease machinery from step 2.

tradeoff: Self-hosted in-memory (Redis/Valkey) gives the cheapest atomic set-if-not-exists with TTL, but loses keys on failover unless persisted.
A managed durable KV/wide-column store (DynamoDB/ElastiCache, Cassandra) survives restarts and scales the dedup window — at higher cost per claim.
PostgreSQL unique constraints work at low volume but become a hotspot at 50k/s.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A store with native conditional writes plus TTL expiry (DynamoDB, Redis) removes the need for a separate cleanup job to age out the dedup window.

tradeoff: Self-hosted relational (PostgreSQL/MySQL) makes the per-recipient delivery state machine and status roll-ups easy with secondary indexes and transactions.
Wide-column (Cassandra/ScyllaDB, or managed DynamoDB/Bigtable) handles the 10M-row-per-broadcast write volume and 30-day retention better, but pushes status aggregation to the read path.
Often a hybrid: relational for notifications, wide-column for deliveries.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A horizontally-scaling managed store (DynamoDB, Spanner, Bigtable) with TTL on rows removes the need to hand-shard deliveries and to build a retention/expiry job.

tradeoff: Preferences are read on every delivery but written rarely, so the source of truth (PostgreSQL/DynamoDB) is fronted by a read-through cache (Redis/ElastiCache).
Self-host the cache for control over invalidation; use managed for zero-ops failover.
The core trade-off is cache freshness vs hot-path latency — short TTL plus event-driven invalidation on opt-out keeps a fresh unsubscribe honored within seconds without hammering the DB.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed KV store with single-digit-ms reads and DAX/MemoryDB-style caching can serve preferences directly, removing a separate cache tier for moderate scale.

tradeoff: Self-hosted Redis/Valkey does the atomic increment-and-check-with-expiry a token bucket needs in one round trip (Lua or native commands).
An embedded in-memory cluster (Hazelcast) avoids a network hop but couples limiter state to app nodes.
Managed Redis (ElastiCache/MemoryDB, Azure Cache) gives HA failover for free — and the store must be highly available because it's on every send's hot path.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed Redis with persistence and cluster failover removes the need to build your own replicated counter store and digest-window flushing.

tradeoff: Integrate APNs/FCM directly for full control over token management, priority headers, and per-message cost.
Use an aggregator (SNS, Firebase Cloud Messaging, Notification Hubs) to hit iOS/Android/Web behind one API and offload token registration.
The aggregator costs you a thinner feature surface and another vendor in the receipt path.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A push aggregator that manages device registrations and per-platform formatting can remove much of the contact-point/device-token lifecycle code.

tradeoff: Running your own SMTP (Postfix) is cheapest at volume, but you own deliverability, IP warmup, and reputation.
Managed senders (SES/SendGrid/Twilio) handle reputation, bounce/complaint webhooks, and carrier routing for you.
SMS is effectively always a vendor (Twilio/SNS); there the choice is single-vendor simplicity vs multi-vendor failover.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed email/SMS sender with built-in bounce/complaint webhooks and suppression lists removes much of the receipt-ingestion and contact-point-pruning work in the callback path.

tradeoff: A workflow engine (self-hosted Temporal/Airflow, or managed Step Functions) gives durable, resumable fanout expansion and scheduled/recurring sends with checkpoints for free.
A lighter task queue (Celery, Cloud Tasks) means you hand-roll the cursor/checkpoint and resume logic yourself.
Heavyweight orchestration can be overkill if fanout is the only long-running job.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable workflow engine with built-in checkpointing and timers removes the hand-built fanout cursor and a separate scheduler for local-time sends.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability (queue age, provider errors, callback lag)
tradeoff: Self-hosted Prometheus/Grafana/OTel gives full control over the custom metrics that matter here — oldest-message age per lane, per-provider error rate, receipt-reconciliation lag — at the cost of running the stack.
Managed APM (CloudWatch, Cloud Monitoring, Azure Monitor) removes ops but can get expensive at high cardinality (per-tenant, per-provider labels).
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed metrics+tracing backend removes the need to run and scale your own time-series database and trace store as label cardinality grows.

patterns.name: Work queue
patterns.what: Decouple accept from deliver with a durable queue and pull-based workers.
patterns.whenToUse: When the producer is fast and bursty but downstream delivery is slow or rate-limited.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read
patterns.what: Materialize a copy per recipient up front (write) or resolve recipients at send time (read).
patterns.whenToUse: Choose by recipient count and fanout frequency — big topics favor fanout-on-read.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried events so a user is notified at most once per logical event.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once pipelines where producers and workers both retry.

patterns.name: Dead-letter queue
patterns.what: Route messages that exhaust retries to a separate queue for inspection instead of dropping or blocking.
patterns.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer where some messages are permanently bad.

patterns.name: Preference / policy filter
patterns.what: Apply user opt-outs, categories, and quiet hours before delivery.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever users must control what reaches them — almost always for notifications.

patterns.name: Rate limiting / throttling
patterns.what: Cap notifications per user and smooth bursts so users aren't spammed and providers aren't overwhelmed.

patterns.name: Provider abstraction / adapter
patterns.what: A uniform channel interface behind per-provider adapters with their own retry/failover.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple interchangeable downstreams (APNs/FCM, multiple email/SMS vendors).

patterns.name: Sharded queues
patterns.what: Partition the queue so throughput scales and one hot tenant can't starve others.
patterns.whenToUse: When a single queue/consumer group becomes the throughput bottleneck.

steps.title: 1. Synchronous Single-Channel Send (baseline)
steps.description: Start minimal: a service calls the notification API, which calls a provider (say push) synchronously and returns the result. This surfaces the core tension — delivery depends on a slow, flaky third party — before we add machinery.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens to the caller when the push provider is slow, down, or rate-limiting you?

concepts.definition: An external delivery service (APNs/FCM for push, an email/SMS vendor).
concepts.whyItMatters: You don't control its latency or uptime, so synchronous coupling is fragile.

concepts.definition: 'Sent' means handed to the provider; 'delivered' means it reached the device — often confirmed later, out of band.
concepts.whyItMatters: Status is inherently asynchronous even here: the final outcome can only arrive after the request returns, which already pushes the design toward queues and a receipt path.

concepts.definition: The gap between the provider accepting a send and the DB recording it. If the process dies in between, the message was sent but recorded as not-sent.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the root reason at-most-once is unprovable and why later retries must be idempotent — the ambiguity exists from the very first design.

talkingPoints: State the read/write profile and that bursty fanout is the real load, not steady traffic.
Point out the caller is blocked on a third party — unacceptable at 50k/s; one slow provider exhausts the request thread/connection pool and takes down unrelated traffic.
Note the write-after-send crash window: even this naive design can't guarantee status is accurate, which motivates idempotency later.

traps.trap: Calling the provider synchronously on the request path
traps.why: A slow or down provider blocks callers, exhausts threads, and drops notifications under load.
traps.instead: Accept fast, enqueue, and deliver asynchronously with retries.

bottlenecks.issue: Provider latency and outages propagate straight back to callers.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Introduce a queue and workers (next step).

strong: Names provider flakiness as the dominant failure mode.
weak: Assumes providers are fast and reliable.
Blocks the caller until delivery confirms.

whyNow: A concrete baseline makes each later addition a response to a real failure.

view.caption: The client service calls the notification API synchronously, which delivers to the provider and records status in the notifications DB.

steps.title: 2. Queue + Workers (reliable async delivery)
steps.description: Put a durable queue between accept and deliver. The API enqueues and returns 202 immediately; pull-based workers deliver with retries and backoff. Permanently failing messages go to a dead-letter queue rather than blocking or vanishing.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do workers retry without amplifying load during a provider outage, and where do poison messages go?

concepts.definition: The queue may redeliver a message, so a notification can be processed more than once.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the reliable default, but forces deduplication downstream.

concepts.definition: Increasing, randomized retry delays.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents synchronized retry storms that hammer a recovering provider.

concepts.definition: A side queue for messages that exhaust retries. Operating it is a loop: alert on arrivals, inspect, fix root cause, then replay or discard-with-audit — not a place messages rot.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps poison messages from blocking the main queue while preserving them for inspection.

concepts.definition: A pulled message is leased (invisible) for a timeout; the worker acks only after the provider attempt and status write succeed. No ack → the lease expires → redelivery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Acking before the send completes loses messages on a crash; acking after is what makes the queue at-least-once instead of at-most-once. It's also the direct source of duplicates that step 3 dedupes.

concepts.definition: A cap on retries-in-flight per provider plus a breaker that trips on sustained failure, so a downstream outage doesn't multiply into a retry storm.
concepts.whyItMatters: Naive per-message retries during an outage can 10× the load on an already-struggling provider and delay healthy traffic.

options.name: Pull-based workers from a durable queue (default)
options.description: Accepted notifications land in a durable queue, and worker pools pull from it at their own pace to call providers, with retries and a DLQ on the queue.
pros: Absorbs bursts; workers consume at their own pace.
Natural backpressure — the queue grows instead of dropping.
Retries and DLQ are straightforward.
cons: Adds a queue to operate and monitor (lag, depth).

view.caption: The API enqueues onto a durable message queue from which delivery workers pull at their own pace.

options.name: Push-based fan to workers (no queue)
options.description: The API pushes each accepted notification straight to a worker for immediate delivery, with no buffering queue between them.
pros: Lower latency in the happy path.
cons: No buffer — bursts overload workers and providers.
Backpressure and retries become ad-hoc and lossy.

view.caption: The API pushes each notification directly to a delivery worker with no queue between them.

flows.name: Deliver with retry, then DLQ
flows.note: Transient failures retry with backoff; permanent ones dead-letter.

traps.trap: Retrying everything forever
traps.why: Permanently bad messages (invalid token) retry endlessly, wasting capacity and blocking the queue.
traps.instead: Cap retries; route exhausted messages to a DLQ and stop.

traps.trap: Fixed-interval retries with no jitter
traps.why: All workers retry in lockstep and hammer the recovering provider.
traps.instead: Exponential backoff with jitter.

failureDrills.scenario: The push provider is down for 10 minutes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No accepted message is intentionally dropped; delivery resumes on recovery. (Messages that expire or are provider-rejected end as failed, not delivered — 'not lost' means accounted for, not 'all delivered'.)
failureDrills.mitigation: Messages stay queued and retry with backoff; a retry budget / circuit breaker avoids a storm on recovery; queue depth and oldest-message age alert ops.

strong: Adds backoff+jitter and a DLQ without being asked.
Treats the queue as the buffer that gives backpressure.
weak: Drops messages when workers fall behind.

recap.after: Accept is fast; delivery is reliable, retried, and buffered.

whyNow: Reliable async delivery is the backbone everything else builds on.

view.caption: The API accepts the request, returns 202, and enqueues it; a delivery worker pulls from the queue, calls the provider, records status in the DB, and dead-letters on give-up.

steps.title: 3. Idempotency & Deduplication (notify once)
steps.description: At-least-once queues and retrying producers mean the same logical event can arrive twice. An idempotency key on accept and a dedup check on deliver ensure a user is notified at most once per event within a window.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you dedupe — at accept, at deliver, or both — what's the dedup key granularity, and how big is the window?

concepts.definition: A client-supplied key identifying one logical event so retries are recognized. The accept-time key dedupes producer retries; the delivery-time dedup key is finer-grained, typically (notification_id, recipient_id, channel).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets producers safely retry without creating duplicate notifications, and pins down exactly what 'the same notification' means at each stage.

concepts.definition: The time span over which identical keys are collapsed. Implemented as an atomic set-if-not-exists (claim) with a TTL, so a key is claimed exactly once until it expires.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds dedup storage and defines what 'duplicate' means operationally; the TTL is the trade-off between memory and how long a late retry is still recognized.

concepts.definition: The platform can make its own intended-delivery operation idempotent, but it cannot make a third-party provider deliver exactly once. A worker may crash after the provider accepts a send but before status is recorded; a failover can double-send if the primary timed out yet still delivered.
concepts.whyItMatters: Honest framing: dedup + provider idempotency keys minimize duplicate user-visible effects within a bounded window, but true exactly-once across external channels is not provable.

flows.name: Duplicate event is suppressed
flows.note: Same key at accept short-circuits before enqueue.

traps.trap: Deduping only at accept, not at delivery
traps.why: A worker retry after a crash can still deliver twice even if accept was deduped.
traps.instead: Dedupe the delivery attempt too, keyed by (notification_id, recipient_id, channel).

traps.trap: Server-generated dedup keys
traps.why: A producer retry gets a fresh key and the event is treated as new.
traps.instead: The producer supplies a stable key per logical event.

traps.trap: Claiming exactly-once delivery through third-party providers
traps.why: You can't observe the provider's internal state: a crash between provider-accept and status-write, or a failover after a timeout, can still produce a duplicate user-visible send.
traps.instead: Guarantee idempotent intent and bounded dedup; use provider idempotency keys where supported and reconcile ambiguous outcomes from receipts rather than promising at-most-once.

failureDrills.scenario: A producer retries POST after a timeout it didn't see succeed.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: One intended delivery per (notification, recipient, channel) — no duplicate enqueue, and at most one provider send within the dedup window.
failureDrills.mitigation: Accept dedupes by idempotency key and returns the original notification_id; delivery claims the (notification_id, recipient_id, channel) key before sending.

strong: Dedupes at both accept and deliver.
Defines an explicit dedup window and its storage cost.
weak: Assumes the queue delivers exactly once.

recap.before: Retries could notify a user multiple times.
recap.after: At-most-once effect per logical event within the window.
recap.newRisk: Dedup store sizing and window choice become real decisions.

whyNow: Before fanning out to millions, duplicates must be controlled or they multiply.

view.caption: The client sends an idempotency key; the API claims it in the dedup store before enqueuing, and the worker re-checks the dedup store before delivering to the provider.

options.name: Dedupe at accept and at deliver (default)
options.description: An idempotency key rejects duplicate submissions at the API, and a second dedup check at delivery time catches at-least-once queue redelivery.
pros: An idempotency key on accept rejects duplicate producer submissions before they enter the queue.
A deliver-time dedup check catches at-least-once redelivery from the queue, so the user is notified at most once.
Two guards cover both sources of duplicates (retrying producer and redelivering queue).
cons: Two dedup checks add latency and a shared dedup store on both the accept and deliver paths.
The dedup window must be chosen carefully — too short re-notifies, too long bloats the store.
Dedup store becomes a hot dependency that must scale with traffic.

view.caption: The API claims the idempotency key in the dedup store at accept, and the worker checks it again before delivery, so duplicates are dropped at both stages.

options.name: Dedupe at accept only
options.description: A single idempotency check at the API rejects duplicate submissions, after which the queue and workers run with no further dedup.
pros: Single dedup point on the API; the rest of the pipeline stays simple.
Stops duplicate producer submissions and retried sends cheaply at the edge.
Lower latency than checking again on every delivery.
cons: At-least-once queues can still redeliver a message the worker already sent — duplicate notification.
A worker crash after sending but before acking re-sends with no second guard.
Only safe if delivery itself is naturally idempotent, which providers are not.

view.caption: The API claims the idempotency key in the dedup store before enqueuing, but the worker delivers to the provider without a second check.

options.name: Dedupe at deliver only
options.description: A single dedup check guards the provider call, dropping duplicates just before the user is notified while letting them flow through the queue.
pros: Guards the one place a duplicate actually reaches the user — the provider call.
Catches both producer retries and queue redelivery with a single check.
No accept-path coordination needed; producers can be dumb.
cons: Duplicate events still flow through the queue and consume worker capacity before being dropped.
The dedup check sits on the latency-sensitive delivery path.
A bursty duplicate producer can flood the queue even though nothing is sent twice.

view.caption: The API enqueues without dedup, and only the worker checks the dedup store before delivering to the provider.

steps.title: 4. Fanout (one event, many recipients)
steps.description: Broadcasts and topic notifications target many users. Direct-recipient sends still enqueue per recipient; broadcasts add a target-expansion phase. A fanout service expands one event into per-recipient deliveries: materialize per recipient now (small audiences) vs store the event once and stream recipients in chunks into delivery queues at send time (large audiences). Choose by scale.
steps.decisionPrompt: A topic has 10M subscribers and a creator posts hourly. Do you fan out on write or on read?

concepts.definition: Create a delivery per recipient when the event arrives.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fast reads/sends but expensive for huge audiences and frequent events.

concepts.definition: Store the event once; at delivery time resolve subscribers and stream them in chunks into delivery queues. For notifications this is 'expand lazily in batches', not a user reading a timeline.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap for huge, rarely-active audiences; shifts work to send time and lets you apply current preferences/subscriptions as you expand.

concepts.definition: The fanout worker pages through subscribers in fixed-size chunks, enqueues each recipient idempotently (keyed by notification_id, recipient_id, channel), and records a cursor so a crash resumes mid-list instead of restarting.
concepts.whyItMatters: Restarting a 10M-recipient expansion from zero would re-notify everyone already enqueued; the cursor + idempotent enqueue make fanout itself crash-safe.

options.name: Hybrid: write for small audiences, read for large (default)
options.description: Small audiences get a delivery written per recipient up front, while large topics enqueue once and expand on read at delivery time.
pros: Cheap sends for huge topics; fast for small ones.
Avoids materializing 10M rows per post.
cons: Two code paths; a threshold to tune.

view.caption: The fanout service materializes per-recipient messages onto the queue for small audiences but resolves large audiences from the topics store at send time.

options.name: Always fanout-on-write
options.description: Every event materializes one delivery row per recipient at publish time, so delivery is just draining the resulting queue.
pros: Simple, uniform; delivery is just draining a queue.
cons: A 10M-subscriber post writes 10M deliveries up front — expensive and bursty.

view.caption: The fanout service materializes one message per recipient onto the queue for every broadcast.

traps.trap: Fanning out on write for celebrity-scale topics
traps.why: One post materializes millions of rows in a burst, overloading the queue and DB.
traps.instead: Use fanout-on-read (or hybrid) above a subscriber threshold.

traps.trap: Expanding subscribers synchronously in the API
traps.why: Resolving millions of recipients blocks the request.
traps.instead: Hand off to an async fanout worker that streams recipients into the queue.

traps.trap: Snapshotting recipients at publish time and ignoring opt-outs during a long expansion
traps.why: A 10M expansion takes minutes; a user who unsubscribes mid-fanout still gets the message if you resolved preferences only at the start.
traps.instead: Resolve subscriptions as you page, and apply preferences at delivery time (step 5) so a late opt-out is still honored.

traps.trap: Expanding faster than workers can drain
traps.why: Unthrottled fanout floods the queue, ballooning oldest-message age and delaying urgent traffic.
traps.instead: Apply backpressure — gate expansion rate on queue depth/age so fanout slows when delivery falls behind.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout worker crashes halfway through a 10M expansion.
failureDrills.mitigation: Checkpoint progress (cursor) and enqueue idempotently per (notification, recipient, channel) so resume is idempotent.

strong: Picks the fanout strategy by audience size and frequency.
weak: Uses one fanout strategy for all cases.
Expands recipients on the request path.

recap.after: One event expands to per-recipient deliveries at any scale.
recap.newRisk: Huge fanouts are bursty; expansion must be async, checkpointed, and idempotent.

whyNow: Fanout is the load multiplier; everything downstream must tolerate its bursts.

view.caption: The API hands a broadcast to the fanout service, which expands subscribers from the topics store and enqueues one per-recipient message for workers to deliver.

flows.name: One event fans out to many recipients
flows.note: The fanout service expands a topic event into per-recipient deliveries and enqueues each; the API returns immediately.

steps.title: 5. User Preferences & Quiet Hours
steps.description: Insert a preference/policy filter before delivery: per-channel, per-category opt-outs, quiet hours, and channel fallback — plus the harder layer above product preferences: legal consent and provider suppression. Suppressed deliveries are recorded with a reason, not silently dropped.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where in the pipeline do you apply preferences, and how do you keep that check fast at 50k/s?

concepts.definition: A per-user window during which non-urgent notifications are held, digested, or suppressed — evaluated in the user's local time zone, not the server's.
concepts.whyItMatters: Respecting them is the difference between useful and spammy; getting the time zone wrong sends a 'quiet' notification at 3am.

concepts.definition: Users opt out of a category (marketing) and/or a channel (SMS) independently.
concepts.whyItMatters: Real consent is multi-dimensional, not a single on/off switch.

concepts.definition: Three distinct layers: product preference (user's chosen settings), legal consent (a regulated opt-out for marketing email/SMS that must be auditable), and provider suppression (a hard block from a bounce/complaint/invalid token). They are checked together but governed differently.
concepts.whyItMatters: Urgent/security messages may bypass quiet hours and product preferences, but they must NOT bypass a legal opt-out or a hard suppression for that channel/category — conflating them is a compliance bug.

deepDives.title: Keeping preference checks fast
points: Cache preferences in the worker (read-through) with short TTL; opt-outs are read far more than written.
Invalidate the cache on opt-out — an event-driven bust (or very short TTL) so a fresh unsubscribe is honored within seconds, not after a stale TTL.
Always honor urgent/transactional categories (OTP, security) regardless of marketing opt-outs — but never override a legal opt-out or hard suppression for that channel.
Quiet-hours outcome is a policy choice: defer to after the window, roll into a digest, or suppress — evaluated in the user's local time zone.
Record suppression with a reason so senders can see 'why didn't my notification arrive'.

traps.trap: Checking preferences only at accept time
traps.why: Preferences can change between accept and delivery (often minutes later for big fanouts).
traps.instead: Apply the policy filter at delivery time, close to the send.

traps.trap: Treating quiet hours as a hard drop for everything
traps.why: Security alerts and OTPs must pierce quiet hours.
traps.instead: Classify by category; urgent categories bypass quiet hours (but not legal opt-outs).

traps.trap: Letting an urgent override bypass legal consent or suppression
traps.why: 'Always deliver urgent' is fine for quiet hours and product prefs, but sending to a legally-unsubscribed or hard-suppressed address is a compliance violation.
traps.instead: Layer the checks: urgent bypasses quiet hours/preferences, but legal consent and provider suppression are hard gates for that channel/category.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Fail safe — don't spam, don't lose, don't violate consent.
failureDrills.mitigation: For non-urgent categories, hold/retry; for urgent, fall back to a conservative default (deliver) and log — but still honor cached legal opt-outs/suppression rather than blindly sending.

strong: Applies preferences at delivery time and caches reads.
Separates urgent categories from quiet hours.

recap.after: Users control channels, categories, and timing; urgent always gets through.
recap.newRisk: The preference check is now on the hot path — must be cached and fail-safe.

whyNow: Not spamming users is a core requirement, applied right before sending.

view.caption: The worker checks the preference service (backed by the preferences store) before routing allowed notifications to the provider via the channel router, recording suppressed ones in the DB.

options.name: Preference service before delivery (default)
options.description: A dedicated preference service is called on the delivery path to apply opt-outs, quiet hours, and fallback, recording suppressed sends rather than dropping them.
pros: A dedicated preference/policy service applies per-channel and per-category opt-outs, quiet hours, and fallback in one place.
Suppressed deliveries are recorded as 'suppressed', not silently dropped, for auditability.
Centralized policy is easy to evolve without touching every producer.
cons: A synchronous policy lookup sits on the delivery path and must stay fast at 50k/s.
The preference store/service is a new dependency that must be highly available.
Hot reads require caching preferences to hit the latency budget.

view.caption: The worker queries the dedicated preference service and its store before routing allowed messages to the provider, logging suppressed ones to the DB.

options.name: Inline preference check in the worker
options.description: Each worker reads a cached preferences view and applies opt-out and quiet-hours logic itself, with no separate preference service hop.
pros: No extra service hop: the worker reads a cached preferences view and decides locally.
Lowest latency on the hot delivery path.
Fewer moving parts to operate.
cons: Every worker must embed and update policy logic — duplicated and easy to drift.
A locally cached preferences view can be stale, sending to an opted-out user.
Cross-cutting policy changes require redeploying workers.

view.caption: The worker does a cached preferences lookup itself, delivering to the provider if allowed and recording suppressed ones in the DB.

options.name: Filter at accept time
options.description: Preferences are resolved at the API before the event enters the queue, so opted-out notifications are filtered out before fanout multiplies them.
pros: Opted-out notifications never enter the queue, saving downstream capacity.
The decision is made once at the edge, before fanout multiplies the event.
Keeps delivery workers simple and policy-free.
cons: Quiet hours computed at accept can be wrong by deliver time for delayed/queued sends.
Per-recipient preferences must be resolved before fanout, which is expensive for broadcasts.
Late preference changes (user opts out after enqueue) aren't respected.

view.caption: The API checks the preference service and store at accept time and only enqueues allowed notifications for workers.

steps.title: 6. Multi-Channel Routing & Providers
steps.description: Generalize delivery across channels via a router and per-provider adapters. Each channel (push/email/SMS) has its own provider, retry profile, and failover. Templates render channel-appropriate content. Crucially, delivery is a two-way conversation: providers report final status asynchronously (receipts, bounces, invalid tokens), so this step also adds the callback path that closes the loop on 'delivered'.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you add a second SMS vendor for failover without touching the rest of the pipeline — and how do you learn whether a message was actually delivered?

concepts.definition: A uniform interface implemented per provider (APNs, FCM, SendGrid, Twilio).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you add/replace/fail over providers without changing workers.

concepts.definition: Routing to a secondary provider when the primary fails or degrades.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single vendor outage shouldn't stop a whole channel.

concepts.definition: An asynchronous event from a provider that updates a delivery's real outcome: a push acceptance, an email delivered/bounced/complaint webhook, an SMS carrier DLR. A receipt worker correlates it by provider_message_id and advances the delivery state machine.
concepts.whyItMatters: 'Sent to provider' is not 'delivered'. Without ingesting receipts the system can never report a truthful final status, and bounces/invalid tokens never feed suppression.

concepts.definition: Per-provider health tracking that trips a breaker on sustained errors or timeouts, shedding traffic to a secondary instead of hammering a degraded vendor.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents a slow vendor from consuming all workers and turning one channel's outage into a pipeline-wide stall (retry storm).

concepts.definition: The push token, email, or phone number per user/channel, with health. APNs/FCM and email bounces feed back 'invalid' or 'complained', which prunes the contact point so future sends skip or re-route it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Dead tokens and bounced addresses waste provider quota, hurt sender reputation, and can get you throttled or blocklisted.

concepts.definition: Channel- and locale-specific content pinned to a version. A worker renders the version captured when the notification was accepted.
concepts.whyItMatters: A mid-flight edit to a template shouldn't change what a 10M-recipient broadcast already in flight says.

flows.name: SMS provider failover
flows.note: Primary degrades; router fails over to secondary.

flows.name: Async provider receipt closes the loop
flows.note: The worker only knows 'accepted by provider'. The true outcome arrives later as a callback.

traps.trap: Hardcoding one provider per channel into the worker
traps.why: Adding failover or swapping vendors means rewriting workers and risking regressions.
traps.instead: Put providers behind a uniform adapter interface the router selects at runtime.

traps.trap: Rendering content once for all channels
traps.why: An SMS isn't an email isn't a push payload (length, format, links).
traps.instead: Render per channel via templates with channel-specific variants.

traps.trap: Treating 'sent to provider' as the final delivered status
traps.why: Providers accept first and report the real outcome later; without a callback path, 'delivered' is a guess and bounces never reach suppression.
traps.instead: Ingest provider receipts via a webhook endpoint and reconcile them into the delivery state machine by provider_message_id.

traps.trap: Ignoring provider feedback loops
traps.why: Repeatedly sending to invalid push tokens or bounced emails burns quota and wrecks sender reputation, getting you throttled or blocklisted.
traps.instead: Feed bounces, complaints, and invalid-token events back into contact-point health and suppress dead endpoints.

traps.trap: Treating a primary timeout as proof of non-delivery before failing over
traps.why: A vendor can time out the response yet still deliver; blind failover then double-sends.
traps.instead: Use provider idempotency keys on failover where supported, and reconcile from receipts rather than assuming the primary failed.

failureDrills.scenario: Primary email vendor has elevated bounce rates.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Traffic shifts to a healthy vendor without losing messages; bounced addresses are suppressed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Circuit-breaker health routing with failover; bounce receipts feed back into contact-point suppression.

failureDrills.scenario: A push provider returns many invalid-token errors after an OS update.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Invalid tokens are marked and skipped, not retried forever.
failureDrills.mitigation: Receipt worker prunes contact_points to 'invalid'; workers skip suppressed endpoints.

strong: Abstracts providers behind adapters and routes by health (circuit breaker).
Renders channel-specific content via versioned templates.
Closes the loop with a provider callback/receipt path and feeds bounces into suppression.
weak: Couples workers to a specific vendor.
Treats 'sent' as 'delivered' and never ingests receipts.

recap.before: One channel, one provider, status guessed at send time.
recap.after: Many channels, swappable providers with health-aware failover and per-channel templates, plus an async receipt path that reconciles true delivery status and prunes dead contact points.
recap.newRisk: More providers and a callback stream mean more health signals, suppression handling, and receipt-reconciliation lag to manage.

whyNow: Multi-channel is a core requirement; doing it cleanly needs an abstraction, not if/else per vendor.
Status tracking is in scope from the start — this is where the provider feedback loop that makes 'delivered' truthful actually lands.

view.caption: The worker renders via the template service and hands the message to the channel router, which dispatches to APNs/FCM, email, or SMS providers and dead-letters exhausted ones. Providers later call back the Receipt API; the receipt worker reconciles true status into the DB and suppresses bounced/invalid contact points.

options.name: Router + per-provider adapters (default)
options.description: A channel router fronts pluggable per-provider adapters, dispatching each notification to the right vendor behind one delivery interface with per-channel retry profiles.
pros: A channel router fronts pluggable adapters, so adding a vendor is a new adapter, not a pipeline rewrite.
Each channel keeps its own retry profile and failover independently.
Templates render channel-appropriate content behind one delivery interface.
cons: The router plus adapter set is more abstraction than a single hard-coded provider call.
A shared router is a common path whose failure affects every channel.
Per-channel config (retries, rate, templates) multiplies operational surface.

view.caption: The worker templates the message and the channel router fans it to per-provider adapters for push, email, and SMS, dead-lettering exhausted deliveries.

options.name: Multi-vendor failover per channel
options.description: Each channel routes to a primary vendor and falls over to a secondary on errors or rate limits, all configured behind the same router.
pros: Each channel routes to a primary vendor and fails over to a secondary on errors or rate limits.
A single vendor outage degrades gracefully instead of dropping that channel.
Adding the second SMS vendor is config behind the same router — no pipeline change.
cons: Vendors differ in features and delivery semantics, complicating the adapter abstraction.
Failover must avoid double-sending the same notification across vendors.
More vendor relationships, credentials, and health checks to operate.

view.caption: The channel router sends SMS to a primary provider and fails over to a secondary on error, dead-lettering once both are exhausted.

options.name: Single multi-channel provider
options.description: All channels are handled through one external delivery platform via a single adapter, offloading push/email/SMS specifics to the provider.
pros: Offload channel handling to one provider (e.g. a delivery platform) with a single integration.
Least code to build and operate — one adapter, one contract.
Provider handles push/email/SMS specifics and some failover for you.
cons: Hard vendor lock-in; their outage takes down all channels at once.
Less control over per-channel retry, failover, and cost optimization.
Adding a custom or cheaper vendor for one channel is no longer simple.

view.caption: The worker templates the message and hands it to one multi-channel provider for any channel, dead-lettering exhausted deliveries.

steps.title: 7. Rate Limiting & Throttling
steps.description: Cap notifications per user (don't spam) and smooth bursts toward providers (respect their limits). Limits apply at several scopes — per user, per tenant, per category — with stricter caps on marketing than transactional/security. Combine token-bucket limits, digest/collapse for chatty categories, and provider-side throttling, all backed by a fast shared counter store.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop a buggy producer from notifying one user 500 times in a minute — and where does the over-limit traffic actually go?

concepts.definition: A per-key limiter that refills at a steady rate and allows bursts up to a cap. The key can be per-user, per-tenant, or per-(user, category).
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds volume while tolerating short bursts; the choice of key decides what you're protecting against (user spam vs a noisy tenant vs marketing fatigue).

concepts.definition: Independent limits stacked: a per-user cap (don't fatigue one person), a per-tenant quota (one customer can't consume the platform — ties into the step-8 noisy-tenant problem), and per-category rules (marketing strict, transactional/security loose).
concepts.whyItMatters: A single global or per-user limit can't express 'this tenant is over quota' or 'OTPs are never rate-limited but marketing is' — real systems need all three.

concepts.definition: Over-limit notifications aren't simply dropped — each has a defined outcome: defer (send after the window), digest (collapse into a roll-up), suppress (record with reason), or DLQ (only for genuinely undeliverable). These are different product decisions, not interchangeable.
concepts.whyItMatters: Silently dropping loses information; the disposition determines whether the user eventually gets the content and whether the sender can explain a missing notification.

concepts.definition: Coalescing many notifications into one summary, accumulated in a per-(user, category) window in the counter store and flushed on a timer or threshold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns spam into a useful roll-up for chatty categories.

options.name: Per-user token bucket + provider throttle (default)
options.description: A per-user token bucket in a shared store caps how often each recipient is messaged, layered with a provider-quota throttle on outbound sends.
pros: Caps per-user spam and respects provider quotas.
Bursts allowed up to a cap, then smoothed.
cons: Distributed counters need a fast shared store (e.g. Redis).

view.caption: The worker consults a token-bucket limiter (per user/tenant/category) backed by a shared counter store, which only lets under-limit messages through to the channel router.

options.name: Global fixed-rate throttle only
options.description: A single global rate cap limits total outbound sends, with no per-user accounting.
pros: Trivial to implement.
cons: Doesn't stop spamming a single user.
Either too loose globally or too strict for everyone.

view.caption: The worker pushes messages to the channel router at a single fixed global rate.

traps.trap: Only limiting globally, not per user
traps.why: A bug or abusive sender can still bombard one user while staying under the global cap.
traps.instead: Limit per user (and per category), plus a global/provider throttle.

traps.trap: Dropping over-limit notifications silently
traps.why: Users miss things and senders can't tell why.
traps.instead: Give over-limit traffic a defined disposition — defer, digest, or suppress-with-reason — and record the decision.

traps.trap: Rate-limiting urgent/security notifications the same as marketing
traps.why: An OTP or security alert blocked by a marketing-fatigue cap is worse than spam.
traps.instead: Make limits category-aware: urgent/security bypass user spam caps within carefully bounded rules; marketing is strict.

failureDrills.scenario: A producer bug emits the same alert 500x/min for one user.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The user sees at most a small, bounded number (or a digest).
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-user token bucket caps delivery; excess is collapsed and the anomaly is alerted.

strong: Limits at multiple scopes — user, tenant, category — not just globally.
Gives over-limit traffic a defined disposition (defer/digest/suppress) instead of dropping.
Backs counters with a fast shared store and keeps urgent categories exempt.
weak: Relies on a single global rate.
Rate-limits OTPs the same as marketing.

recap.before: A bad producer could spam a user unboundedly.
recap.after: Per-user / per-tenant / per-category limits with a defined disposition for excess (defer, digest, suppress), backed by a shared counter store.
recap.newRisk: Limiter state is shared and hot — needs a fast, available counter store; the per-tenant scope foreshadows the noisy-tenant problem in step 8.

whyNow: With multi-channel fanout live, abuse and provider-limit breaches are the next real risk.

view.caption: The worker checks the rate limiter (backed by a fast counter/digest store) before routing allowed messages to the provider via the router, recording collapsed or suppressed ones in the DB.

steps.title: 8. Scaling the Pipeline
steps.description: Two independent axes fix the final bottleneck. First, priority lanes (urgent / normal / bulk) keep a marketing blast from head-of-line-blocking an OTP. Second, inside each lane, partition by tenant so one noisy tenant can't starve others. A fair scheduler allocates worker capacity across tenants and lanes; workers autoscale on queue depth and oldest-message age, and queue age applies backpressure to fanout and producer admission.
steps.decisionPrompt: A single tenant's marketing blast is starving everyone else's notifications. What do you change?

concepts.definition: Two orthogonal axes. The lane (urgent / normal / bulk) decides scheduling precedence; the partition key (tenant, then maybe user-hash) decides isolation and parallelism within a lane.
concepts.whyItMatters: Conflating them — e.g. 'shard A = urgent, shard B = bulk' — means you can't both prioritize urgent traffic and isolate a noisy tenant. They compose: lane × partition.

concepts.definition: Splitting a lane by a key (tenant, then user-hash) across partitions/consumer groups. Watch for hot partitions (one huge tenant) and rebalance with consistent hashing or sub-partitioning.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scales throughput and isolates noisy tenants — but a single dominant tenant can still create a hot partition that needs splitting.

concepts.definition: Weighted-fair allocation of delivery capacity across tenants (and lanes) so one can't monopolize it — not just static per-tenant queues.
concepts.whyItMatters: Static shards still let an idle tenant's reserved capacity sit unused while a busy one waits; a scheduler reallocates dynamically.

concepts.definition: Using queue depth / oldest-message age as a signal to slow upstream work: throttle fanout expansion and admission control on accept when delivery can't keep up.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, a 10M-recipient blast floods the queue faster than workers drain it, ballooning latency for everyone including urgent traffic.

options.name: Per-tenant partitions + priority lanes (default)
options.description: Traffic is split into per-tenant queue partitions with separate priority lanes, so urgent notifications bypass bulk blasts and noisy tenants stay isolated.
pros: Isolates noisy tenants; urgent lane bypasses bulk blasts.
Scales horizontally by adding partitions.
cons: More partitions to manage; needs autoscaling on depth.

view.caption: Two priority lanes — bulk and urgent — each tenant-partitioned inside, drained by a fair scheduler over the worker pool so urgent never waits behind a bulk blast and no tenant starves another.

options.name: Single queue, scale workers only
options.description: All notifications share one queue and throughput is grown only by adding workers, with no tenant or priority partitioning.
pros: Simplest; no partitioning logic.
cons: One huge blast still head-of-line-blocks everyone.
No tenant isolation.

view.caption: A single queue feeds the delivery workers, scaled out with no lanes or tenant partitioning — simplest, but one blast head-of-line-blocks everyone.

deepDives.title: Lanes, partitions, and fairness
points: Axis 1 — priority lane: separate urgent (OTP/security) from normal and bulk (marketing); urgent is scheduled ahead of bulk.
Axis 2 — partition key: inside each lane, partition by tenant (then user-hash) so a noisy tenant's blast radius is one partition, not the lane.
Fair scheduler: weighted-fair allocation across tenants and lanes, not just static shards — reclaims idle tenants' capacity.
Hot partitions: one dominant tenant can still saturate a partition; sub-partition or consistent-hash rebalance.
Autoscale worker count on queue depth and age of the oldest message (not CPU); feed that same age/depth signal back as backpressure to fanout and admission.
Partition-key trade-off: tenant isolation vs per-user ordering and locality for dedup/rate-limit state — keep a user's deliveries on one partition if you need their dedup/limit counters local.

traps.trap: One global queue for urgent and bulk together
traps.why: A marketing blast head-of-line-blocks time-sensitive OTPs.
traps.instead: Separate priority lanes; urgent never waits behind bulk.

traps.trap: Sharding by a key that doesn't isolate tenants
traps.why: A noisy tenant still spreads across all partitions and starves others.
traps.instead: Partition by tenant (and add fair scheduling) so blast radius is contained.

traps.trap: Collapsing priority lanes and tenant partitions into one set of queues
traps.why: Using 'queue A = urgent, queue B = bulk' as the only split means you've spent both queues on priority and have nothing left to isolate a noisy tenant — they're different axes.
traps.instead: Treat lane and partition key as independent: lane decides precedence, partition key decides isolation; they compose as lane × tenant.

strong: Separates priority lanes from tenant partitioning as two independent axes.
Adds a fair scheduler, not just static shards; handles hot partitions.
Autoscales on queue depth/age and feeds it back as backpressure, not CPU alone.
weak: Conflates 'urgent vs bulk' with 'tenant A vs tenant B'.

recap.before: One queue capped throughput and let tenants interfere.
recap.after: Priority lanes × tenant partitions with fair scheduling and depth/age autoscaling, plus backpressure to fanout and admission.
recap.newRisk: More partitions and lanes to operate; routing keys, hot-partition rebalancing, and the lane/partition trade-off must be chosen well.

whyNow: At 50k/s with bursty fanout, a single queue is the final bottleneck and fairness risk.

view.caption: The API routes by priority into the urgent and bulk lanes, each partitioned by tenant inside; a fair scheduler over the worker pool drains them so urgent bypasses bulk and no tenant starves another, then workers call the provider.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: a fast accept API with idempotency, durable priority lanes partitioned by tenant, async fanout (hybrid write/read), preference- and consent-filtered delivery workers, multi-provider channel routing with health-aware failover and versioned templates, per-user rate limiting with digests, a DLQ for poison messages, and — closing the loop — an async provider-receipt path that reconciles true delivery status and prunes dead contact points.

view.caption: The client calls the API, which dedupes and fans out via the topics store onto tenant-partitioned urgent/bulk lanes; workers apply preferences, rate limits, and templates, then route to push/email/SMS providers (dead-lettering failures) and record sent status in the DB. Providers later call back the Receipt API; the receipt worker reconciles final status and suppresses bounced/invalid contact points.

flows.name: Accept → fan out → deliver → reconcile
flows.note: The full operational path: synchronous accept, async expansion and delivery, then an out-of-band receipt that finalizes status.

messages.label: reconcile final status; suppress dead contact points

functional.how: Fast 202 accept API that enqueues for async delivery.
Channel router with per-provider adapters and templates.
Preference/policy filter applied at delivery time with caching.

functional.requirement: Fan out one event to many recipients
functional.how: Async fanout service, hybrid write/read by audience size.
Per-(notification, recipient, channel) delivery rows updated by workers.

nonFunctional.how: Durable queue, retries with backoff, DLQ for poison messages.
Idempotency at accept and dedup at delivery within a window.
Priority lanes keep OTP/security ahead of bulk blasts.
Sharded queues and autoscaled workers absorb fanout bursts.
Per-user rate limits and digest collapse.
Health-aware routing with failover; backoff and DLQ.

say: Restate it: deliver multi-channel notifications reliably, at scale, without spamming.
List channels, fanout, preferences as functional; reliability, dedup, no-spam, urgent-latency as non-functional.
Call out the headline tension: a slow third-party provider plus huge bursty fanout.
Size peak notifications/s, max recipients per broadcast, and per-channel latency targets.
Sketch POST /notifications (with Idempotency-Key), GET status, preferences, topic subscribe.
Start synchronous, then add the queue+workers backbone with retries and a DLQ.
Layer idempotency/dedup, then fanout, preferences, multi-channel routing, and rate limiting.
Go deep where pushed: fanout strategy, dedup, or fairness/priority lanes.
Shard queues by tenant with priority lanes; autoscale on depth.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the top bottleneck (provider limits / fanout bursts).

expectations: Puts a queue between accept and delivery and explains why.
Knows retries need backoff and that failures shouldn't be dropped.
Designs dedup at accept and delivery; defines a dedup window.
Chooses fanout-on-write vs read by audience size; keeps expansion async.
Applies preferences at delivery with caching and urgent-category bypass.
Isolates tenants with sharded queues and fair scheduling; separates urgent vs bulk lanes.
Designs provider failover and health-aware routing across vendors.
Reasons about blast radius, backpressure, and the cost of each delivery guarantee.

followUps: How do you handle delivery receipts and read-tracking that arrive asynchronously from providers?
How would you implement scheduled and recurring notifications (send at 9am local time)?
How do you manage device-token lifecycle and prune invalid tokens fed back from providers?
How would you add an in-app inbox that must stay consistent with push delivery?
How do you A/B test notification content and measure engagement without double-counting?
How would you enforce global compliance (unsubscribe, GDPR deletion) across all channels and history?

links.groupDescription: Real notification platforms and operational lessons from shipped systems.
links.title: Rebuilding Slack's Notification Experience: A Deep Dive into Architecture and Craftsmanship
links.why: Shows how a large product redesigned notification preferences, rendering, and delivery behavior around user experience and correctness.

links.groupDescription: Real notification platforms and operational lessons from shipped systems.
links.title: Tracing Notifications
links.why: Explains how Slack traces asynchronous notification delivery across services, which is directly useful for debugging queue lag and missing sends.

links.groupDescription: Real notification platforms and operational lessons from shipped systems.
links.title: Air Traffic Controller: Member-First Notifications at LinkedIn
links.why: Good production reference for notification ranking, frequency caps, channel choice, timing, and member-level fatigue controls.

links.groupDescription: Real notification platforms and operational lessons from shipped systems.
links.title: Concourse: Generating Billions of Personalized Notifications at LinkedIn
links.why: Covers large-scale notification generation and fanout, including separating candidate generation from downstream delivery.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains logs, partitions, replication, and consumer groups for high-throughput asynchronous delivery pipelines.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Amazon SQS Visibility Timeout
links.why: Concrete reference for lease-and-ack processing, redelivery after worker failure, and why queues tend toward at-least-once delivery.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Amazon SQS Dead-Letter Queues
links.why: Defines the poison-message escape path used when retries are exhausted without blocking the main delivery lane.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Timeouts, Retries, and Backoff with Jitter
links.why: Practical guidance for making provider retries bounded and randomized so outages do not become retry storms.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Avoiding Insurmountable Queue Backlogs
links.why: Useful for understanding queue age, drain rate, and backpressure when fanout produces more work than workers can consume.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Idempotent Requests
links.why: A concrete API-level model for client-supplied idempotency keys and safe retries.

links.groupDescription: Messaging semantics, retry behavior, duplicate handling, and backlog control.
links.title: Pattern: Idempotent Consumer
links.why: Connects at-least-once messaging to the consumer-side dedup store used before user-visible sends.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Sending Notification Requests to APNs
links.why: Grounds the push-provider integration path, request shape, and external-provider boundary for iOS notifications.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Handling Notification Responses from APNs
links.why: Shows how provider errors and invalid device tokens feed back into contact-point lifecycle and suppression.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Manage FCM Registration Tokens
links.why: Explains stale-token detection and pruning, a core feedback-loop concern for push notification systems.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Best Practices When Sending FCM Messages at Scale
links.why: Covers spikes, quotas, retries, and backoff behavior for large push-notification senders.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Topic Messaging on Android
links.why: A concrete provider-side fanout model for many recipients subscribing to one topic.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Event Webhook
links.why: Shows delivered, bounce, deferred, spam-report, and unsubscribe events that a receipt path must ingest.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Message Status Callbacks
links.why: Defines the asynchronous SMS delivery receipt path and status state transitions after a provider accepts a send.

links.groupDescription: Official provider references for push, email, SMS, callbacks, token health, and throttling.
links.title: Messaging Services: Scaling and Queueing
links.why: Useful for provider throughput limits, queueing latency, and why outbound rate control must be channel-aware.

links.groupDescription: Controls for overload, abuse, observability, and production operation.
links.title: Handling Overload
links.why: Useful for admission control, load shedding, quotas, and graceful degradation under notification spikes.

links.groupDescription: Controls for overload, abuse, observability, and production operation.
links.title: Redis Rate Limiter
links.why: Concrete implementation reference for shared counters that enforce per-user, per-tenant, or per-category notification limits.

links.groupDescription: Controls for overload, abuse, observability, and production operation.
links.title: OpenTelemetry Documentation
links.why: Instrumentation standard for correlating request, queue, worker, provider, and receipt spans in asynchronous delivery.
